pages.full_text:
In the afternoon the boys messed about a boats in the lake & in the swimming pool. Simon went back to [?Cassfarogue] to buy stamps. Simon & Eve swam. Simon also booked supper at __Calvinet__. We set off walking just after 6.30 &

[glued-in business card: Louis Bernard Puech / ARTISAN CUISINIER / 15340 CALVINET / HOTEL BEAUSEJOUR / Tél. 71.49.01.68]

got there just before 7.30. The path was mostly a rutted farm track. A lovely evening, beautiful small hills.
	The meal was delicious, but very slow. We started off with a little "Mise en Bouche" of gazpacho soup. Then I had chicken pâté, hot fish & potatoe mould & coffee icecream with brandy-snaps. The bread was good, the boys kept sending the waitresses back for more water in the small jugs.
	It was [very] nearly dark as we set off for home, & quite dark when & we got home. Charles was frightened of meeting the

[right margin, rotated:] This angel his an ancient Arab inscription on the base of his
photo_captions: Louis Bernard Puech / ARTISAN CUISINIER / 15340 CALVINET / HOTEL BEAUSEJOUR / Tél. 71.49.01.68
pages.notes: Page is a continuation mid-entry; no date heading visible on this page. A glued-in business card/menu card for 'Louis Bernard Puech, Artisan Cuisinier, Hotel Beausejour, 15340 Calvinet' is pasted in. The place name returned for stamps ('Cassfarogue') is uncertain — could be a nearby village or campsite name; the reading is approximate. The word 'very' appears struck through with 'nearly' written above it. The right margin contains rotated text continuing onto the right page. Text breaks off mid-sentence at the bottom ('Charles was frightened of meeting the') and continues on the following spread. The meal described is a restaurant dinner at Calvinet (Louis Bernard Puech). 'potatoe' is the diarist's spelling.

pages.full_text:
[continuing rotated text from left margin:] garment — the day of glory. Really, [?] (illegible). We parked up high & walked down to the abbey. The courtyard in front of it is better to park below & walk up. The courtyard in front of the tympanum is very welcoming

[glued-in postcard: Conques - Détail du Tympan]
pages.notes: The handwritten page number '48' appears at the top-right corner. The left portion of this page contains rotated/diagonal text continuing from the left page's right margin — it describes a visit to Conques abbey (Aveyron, France) and its tympanum. A glued-in postcard captioned 'Conques - Détail du Tympan' is pasted on the right side of the page. The rotated text is slightly difficult to read due to angle and overlapping with the postcard. One word after 'Really,' is illegible. The sentence 'This angel his an ancient Arab inscription on the base of his garment — the day of glory' refers to a detail of the tympanum at Conques. 'his' likely means 'has' (diarist's slip). The rest of the right page below the postcard appears blank.
